text: The surgical field shows a collapsed lung with visible adhesions. Two Versius robotic arms, identifiable by purple and cyan instrument units, are positioned at the top right and top left. The surgeon, assisted by a bedside assistant, is actively working to take down the adhesions using the robotic system.

The surgeon uses a fenestrated grasper in the right hand and a fenestrated Maryland dissector with energy in the left hand to manipulate vascular adhesions. An assistant introduces a harmonic device to aid in haemostasis, ensuring a clear surgical field by minimizing bleeding.

The surgeon uses his right hand to maneuver a rolled-up swab within the surgical field, effectively managing lung collapse with CO₂ without the need for a suction device, which would otherwise remove CO₂ and cause the lung to rise.

Sven uses rolled-up swabs to manage loose adhesions and employs a harmonic device for larger adhesions. He operates with two robotic arms, using a swab with his left arm to pad the lung, preventing air leaks or tears. The Maryland and fenestrated grasper identify the nodule located peripherally on the lung surface at the apex of the right upper lobe, highlighting the importance of removing adhesions for effective surgical access.

The robotic fenestrated grasper lifts the nodule on the right upper lobe of the lung to prepare for stapling. The surgical setup includes two 5mm ports for the robotic arms, a 12mm port for the stapler, and a 10mm port for the endoscope. The bedside assistant fires a Covidien purple tri-stapler 60 to excise the nodule.

The surgeon adjusts the robotic arm positions to optimize movement around the chest, successfully extracting the nodule from the lung surface. The nodule is placed in a retrieval bag, and the incision is slightly extended to facilitate its removal.

The surgeon uses the assistant to retract tissue while performing adhesiolysis, searching for lymph nodes in the subcarinal station. The surgical field reveals numerous adhesions as the team works to gain access to the station seven area.

The surgeon navigates through firm adhesions using two robotic arms, with colored instrument base units visible. A mid-lymph node, likely a station IIIa anterior to the superior vena cava (SVC), is identified and extracted using a glove thumb to prevent port seeding.
